question.text: A put option with a strike price of Rs. 17,500 when the underlying spot price is Rs. 18,000 is:

answer.text: Out-of-the-Money (OTM) because the spot price is above the strike price
In-the-Money (ITM) because the spot price is above the strike price
At-the-Money (ATM) because both values are close to 18,000
In-the-Money because the strike price is a round number

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** For a PUT option: OTM when Spot > Strike. Here Spot (18,000) > Strike (17,500), so the put is OTM. - Put ITM: S < X (spot below strike - put buyer benefits) - Put ATM: S ≈ X - Put OTM: S > X (spot above strike - put has no exercise value)
**The Trap:** Moneyness for puts is OPPOSITE to calls. When a call is ITM, the same-strike put is OTM.
**Cross-Exam Context:** If call with strike X is ITM, put with same strike X is OTM (and vice versa).
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Put ITM: S < X (underlying below strike). Put OTM: S > X (underlying above strike).
